Classes teach you all about advanced topics within CS, from operating systemsto machine learning, but there’s one critical subject that’s rarely covered,and is instead left to students to figure out on their own: proficiency withtheir tools. We’ll teach you how to master the command-line, use a powerfultext editor, use fancy features of version control systems, and much more!
 Students spend hundreds of hours using these tools over the course of theireducation (and thousands over their career), so it makes sense to make theexperience as fluid and frictionless as possible. Mastering these tools notonly enables you to spend less time on figuring out how to bend your tools toyour will, but it also lets you solve problems that would previously seemimpossibly complex.

   如果克里斯托弗斯特林格的22年苹果职业有一个词，它可能是“无名的”。虽然他的名字在大约1,400项专利上，但澳大利亚出生和英国筹集的设计师很少听到Cupertino之外。一个例外情况不是在主题演讲外观中，但在审判他在雇主和三星之间作证的复杂专利纠纷。
   目标是重新思考音频。你听到苹果的视觉设计的神之一就是专注于耳朵。 “那里的感知是人们只是不再关心音频 - 它 - 它＆＃39所以不真实，”他在本周在一个现场演示中告诉我。 （是的，我在纽约市下东方的阁楼里冒出了阁楼，他的公司租来炫耀他的创作。）“人们倾向于精彩的经历。 Syng的目标是民主化的声音，并模糊创造者和倾听者之间的线条。“
  如今，Syng正在宣布首个产品，一个名为Cell Alpha的音乐扬声器。在他寻求赢得耳朵时，纵向仍然没有放弃眼睛。避开通常的箱子美学，或圆柱体的首页替代方案，电池是一个闪亮的未来派球，具有镜子状表面。它可能是保龄球。 （Stringer承认他已经探索了使用Bowling-Ball袋来运输它们。）在播放音乐时，电池的略微平坦的顶部有一个脉冲膜，提供了一点戏剧，带有低音和高音。
  对于我的非听证蓟耳，细胞alpha听起来很棒。流媒体时。来自Spotify的歌曲，它听起来比另外两位高端扬声器更好，它使500美元的Sonos五个高级扬声器声音如晶体管无线电。当你在房子里有三个左右的时候，这种经验最令人印象深刻，但即使是一个单细胞配置似乎也毯子毯子毯子。我提到我们在高端说话吗？细胞alpha为1,800美元的费用（如果您获得带落地的版本，那么170美元）。为民主化而言。
  Stringer表示，细胞APHA只是一个更广泛的产品线的第一个。现在，他确信细胞提供独特的价值，因为它提供了其他人甚至没有想到的声音的维度。为了超越我们当前的SoundScapes并进入空间音频世界，他认为，我们必须超越单声道和立体声进入等待IT-Triphonic。是的，这是弥补的词。 “这不得不发生，”他刚刚发明的旅行社时代的Stringer说，“因为我们试图建立稳定的特殊标准占上风。我们认为我们拥有唯一填补账单的技术。“ 
Stringer指的是混合现实的未来时代，在声音 - 不仅仅是音乐，而且我们听到的一切都必须匹配或超过物理世界中的环境源。他的扬声器的多细胞配置可以以一种复制现场表现的经验，呈现音乐，甚至是戏剧性的性能。基本上，他为您只知道的全息音乐会创造了音乐会。 （如果只有我们在锁定前有那些全阵和细胞。）
 Stringer还向我展示了一些不是初始版本的技巧，但突出了Syng的可能性。一个演示涉及由Stringer＆＃39; S团队能够隔离每个音乐家的字符串四重奏的专门记录的“eleanorigby”版本。使用Slick Cell应用程序，他们向我展示了如何将每个仪器拖放，就像将实际仪器移到厨房门附近的大街上的房间里的不同部位一样。在另一个演示中，Syng员工声学工程师Elisabeth McMullin向我展示了系统如何与其他歌曲（在这种情况下，射线头歌曲）与其他歌曲中的声音集成，甚至脚步声，鸟类或警报器。在这些情况下，Syng基本上在录音室中提供了等效的声音板，您可以在那里可以降低或提高每个轨道上的音量。但是你在太空中移动它而不是使轨道更倾向于或更安静。
 Syng，位于加利福尼亚州的威尼斯，现在拥有大约50名员工，并于到目前为止投资1500万美元。斯特林格上诉致敬，即他的投资者包括代表苹果公司的律师和对方律师的律师。他报告了热门音乐家和生产者的热情反应（他不会透露的名字）。 “现在三年来，我在演示后一直在给予演示，因为我的心脏是激起创造者的激情，”他说。 “这些人需要这样的工具来获得下一个创造力。我们＆＃39;重复听到很多关于在立体声中没有足够的空间来做他们想要的东西。“
 Stringer自己从未如此搅拌。在苹果，他一直在背景中。他说他很好，也许是因为终身不愿意参与公共场所。但是现在，作为一个56岁的首席执行官（尽管他刚从Laurel Canyon Singer-Songwriters的团聚中出现）他感到恢复活力。 “我刚知道我必须做的其他事情，”他说。 “它真的不得不在外面。要提出您想要站在后面的解决方案，您需要在整个过程中参与。你可以＆＃39; t只是一个旅程的一步。它只是这样，你只需要做点什么。这是你的时候＆＃39;舒适足以让人感到不舒服。“
    Christopher Striger在2001年在苹果公司的设计团队中，当公司发布了它的击中音乐播放器，即iPod。 2004年7月，我写了一个新的灵网封面故事，记录产品如何成为自己的文化艺术品：
  达到300万加业主，iPod不仅不断访问他们整个歌曲和CD集合，而且会成员资格成为一个隐含的社会，＆＃39; S转换方式将来会消耗音乐。 ＆＃34;当我的学生用我的iPod看到我的校园里，他们微笑，＆＃34; Katch教授说，他的单位将来自莫扎特的一切存储给Dean Martin。 ＆＃34;它＆＃39; s一种粘合。＆＃34;
  添加到上诉是列表批准的纸质。 ＆＃34;我喜欢它！＆＃34;歌曲作者说富裕。 ＆＃34;我有我的整个目录，我到处都是。＆＃34;她只是一个名人普罗斯特合唱的声音，他们唱着普通的iPod用户称赞的同样赞美，但是为设备添加了一个凉爽的凉爽，好像它需要它。史密斯将借此杰克莱诺和有线杂志关于他与＃34的迷恋;本世纪的小工具。＆＃34; Gwyneth Paltrow将她的Pod-Love向Vogue（她的新婴儿被命名为Apple-Coiccent）......实际创造音乐的人是最大的粉丝：＆＃34;布局提醒音乐家音乐家，＆＃34; Tunester John Mayer说。并且Couture Maven Karl Lagerfeld＆＃39; S iPod集合最多60次，通过激光蚀刻编码，所以他可以讲述什么＆＃39; ＆＃34;它＆＃39;商店音乐的方式，＆＃34;他说。 Lagerfeld＆＃39;对iPod的致敬是1,500美元的Fendi Pink铜矩形钱包，可容纳12个iPod。 
从下降，比尔写道，“现在有两个卢昂的气球在澳大利亚进展。有没有人真的知道发生了什么？“
 条例草案是指以字母表的X部门推出的互联网提供的高空气球。几个月前，X宣布了它的Loon项目结束;但是，有一段时间，它看起来像通过60,000英尺缠绕地球的气球提供互联网的不太可能的前景可能实际上工作。比尔，我很欣赏这个问题，但如果你仔细阅读了普通的看法，当我打破了懒人的消息时，你就会看到，尽管项目结束了，一些气球将需要一段时间才落到地球上。至于是否有人知道发生了什么，显然是x。他们在本周初，三个气球仍然高举三个气球的独家！5月13日星期四下跌。懒人项目的最终气球将在周一来到Terra Firma。幸运的是，当一个懒人气球下降时，这是一个比下降的中国空间碎片更良好，因为这个卡通表现出来。到下一个Moonshot！
  机器学习已经解释了埃克森移动在气候变化中的作用。这是令人印象深刻的令人印象深刻的伎俩吗？

   If there is one word for Christopher Stringer’s 22-year career at Apple, it might be “unsung.” Though his name is on around 1,400 patents, the Australian-born and UK-raised designer was seldom heard of outside Cupertino. The one exception occurred not at a keynote appearance, but at a trial where he testified about a complicated patent dispute between his employer and Samsung.
   The goal is to rethink audio. You heard right—one of Apple’s gods of visual design is focusing on the ear. “There's a perception that people just don't care about audio anymore—it's so not true,” he told me during a live demo this week. (Yes, I ventured out of my bunker to a loft on New York City’s Lower East Side that his company had rented to show off his creation.) “Folks gravitate to wonderful experiences. The goal of Syng is to democratize sound and to blur the lines between creator and listener.”
  Today, Syng is announcing its first product, a music speaker called Cell Alpha. In his quest to win the ear, Stringer certainly hasn’t abandoned the eye. Eschewing the usual boxy aesthetic, or the HomePod-ish alternative of a cylinder, the Cell is a shiny futuristic sphere with a mirrorlike surface. It could be ET’s bowling ball. (Stringer admits that he has explored using bowling-ball bags to transport them.) When playing music, the Cell’s slightly flattened top has a pulsing membrane that provides a bit of drama with bass and treble.
  To my non-audiophile ear, the Cell Alpha sounds great. When streaming an H.E.R. song from Spotify, it sounded way better than two other well-regarded high-end speakers, and it made the $500 Sonos Five premium speaker sound like a transistor radio. The experience is most impressive when you’ve got three or so in the house, but even a single-cell configuration seems to blanket a room with crisp sound. Did I mention that we are talking high-end? The Cell Alpha costs $1,800 a unit ($170 more if you get the version with a floor stand). So much for democratization.
  Stringer says the Cell Apha is only the first of what will be a broader product line. For now he’s convinced that Cell provides unique value because it delivers a dimension of sound that others haven’t even thought of. To move beyond our current soundscapes and enter the world of spatial audio, he argues, we must move beyond the monophonic and stereophonic into—wait for it— triphonic. Yes, that’s a word Syng made up. “That had to happen,” says Stringer of the triphonic era he just invented, “because we’re trying to establish the stable special standard that prevails. We think we have the only technology that fills the bill.”
 Stringer is referring to the coming age of mixed reality where sound—not just music, but everything we hear—will have to match or exceed the ambient sources of sound in the physical world. A multicellular configuration of his speakers can present music, or even a theatrical performance, in a way that replicates the experience of a live performance. Essentially, he’s creating the soundtrack for the holographic concerts that you just know are coming. (If only we would have had those holographs and Cells before lockdown.)
 Stringer also showed me some tricks that aren’t part of the initial release, but highlight Syng’s possibilities. One demo involved a specially recorded version of “Eleanor Rigby” by a string quartet where Stringer's team was able to isolate each musician. Using the slick Cell app, they showed me how you could drag and drop each instrument as if moving the actual instruments to different parts of the room—violin on the couch, cello near the kitchen door. In another demo, Syng staff acoustic engineer Elisabeth McMullin showed me how the system could integrate sounds from a recording (in this case, a Radiohead song) with other songs, or even sound effects like footsteps, birds, or sirens. In these cases, Syng is essentially providing the equivalent of a soundboard in a recording studio, where you can lower or raise the volume on each track. But instead of making the track louder or quieter, you’re moving it in space.
 Syng, located in Venice, California, now has about 50 employees, and funders have invested $15 million so far. It’s a tribute to Stringer’s appeal that his investors include both the lawyer representing Apple in that patent suit and the opposing attorney as well. He reports enthusiastic responses from top musicians and producers (whose names he won’t reveal). “For three years now I've been giving demo after demo because my heart is to stir the passions of creators,” he says. “These people need tools like this to get to the next level of creativity. We're hearing a lot about how there's just not enough space in stereo to do what they want.”
 Stringer himself has never been so stirred. At Apple he’d always been in the background. He says that he was fine with it, perhaps because of a life-long reluctance to engage in public venues. But now, as a 56-year-old CEO (albeit one who looks like he just emerged from a reunion of Laurel Canyon singer-songwriters) he feels rejuvenated. “I just knew that there was something else I had to do,” he says. “It really had to be outside. To put out a solution that you want to stand behind, you need to be participating throughout the entire process. You can't just be a step in a journey. It just had to be this, you just had to make something. It’s when you're comfortable enough to get uncomfortable.”
    Christopher Stringer was on Apple’s design team in 2001 when the company released its hit music player, the iPod. In July 2004, I wrote a  Newsweek cover story documenting how the product had become a cultural artifact of its own:
  To 3 million-plus owners, iPods not only give constant access to their entire collection of songs and CDs, but membership into an implicit society that's transforming the way music will be consumed in the future. "When my students see me on campus with my iPod, they smile," says Professor Katch, whose unit stores everything from Mozart to Dean Martin. "It's sort of a bonding."
  Adding to the appeal is the cachet of A-list approbation. "I love it!" says songwriter Denise Rich. "I have my whole catalog on it, and I take it everywhere." She is only one voice in a chorus of celebrity Podsters who sing the same praises voiced by ordinary iPod users, but add a dollop of coolness to the device, as if it needed it. Will Smith has burbled to Jay Leno and WIRED  magazine about his infatuation with "the gadget of the century." Gwyneth Paltrow confided her Pod-love to Vogue  (her new baby is named Apple—coincidence?) … People who actually create music are among the biggest fans: "The layout reminds the musician of music," says tunester John Mayer. And couture maven Karl Lagerfeld's iPod collection is up to 60, coded in the back by laser etching so he can tell what's on them. "It's the way to store music," he says. Lagerfeld's tribute to the iPod is a $1,500 Fendi pink copper rectangular purse that holds 12 iPods.
   From down under, Bill writes, “There are now two of Loon’s balloons progressing over Australia. Does anyone actually know what’s going on?”
 Bill is referring to the internet-providing high-altitude balloons launched by Alphabet’s X division. A few months ago, X announced  the end of its Loon project; but for a while, it looked like the unlikely prospect of providing internet by balloons circling the globe at 60,000 feet might actually work. Bill, I appreciate the question, but if you’d read the Plain View carefully when  I broke the news that Loon had popped, you would have seen that though the project was over, some balloons would take a while to fall to earth. As for whether anyone knows what’s going on, apparently X does. They told me—in an exclusive!—that of the three balloons still aloft at the beginning of this week, two were coming down on Thursday, May 13. The final balloon of the Loon project would be on terra firma this coming Monday. Fortunately, when a Loon balloon comes down, it’s a much more benign event than falling  Chinese space debris, as  this cartoon demonstrates. On to the next moonshot!

Facebook正在Whatsapp用户推动一个神秘和积极的“隐私更新”。这就是为什么
   我是连接世界上四分之一的人口的消息传递应用程序，但许多美国人还没有听说过whatsapp。这是因为美国大多数手机计划提供标准的统一费率，以便允许人们在国家内自由沟通。但是整个世界上的大部分地区，包括许多世界上最贫穷的国家，人们被收取他们发送和接收的每条消息。
 这就是为什么自2009年推出以来，Whatsapp已成为数十亿人的重要资源 - 他们准备捍卫它。当黎巴嫩政府试图带来“WhatsApp税”时，每天收取0.20美元的呼叫在应用程序上进行的呼叫，它有助于触发2019年席卷该国的大众抗议活动。
  然而，将美国人连接到Whatsapp用户的一件事是Facebook首席执行官标记Zuckerberg以及他的公然无视数据隐私。 Facebook在2014年在2014年获得WhatsApp，以巩固对全球通信的控制。现在，Zuckerberg正在前进，改变了Whatsapp的隐私政策，旨在将我们的通信商业化，以便为Facebook的贪得无厌的贪婪提供。
 在WhatsApp在2014年购买时，该应用程序没有收集电话号码，元数据或其他联系信息。 Facebook承诺保持这种方式。 “我们绝对不会改变WhatsApp周围的计划，并以其使用用户数据的方式，”Zuckerberg声称。 “Whatsapp将全神贯注。”
 然而，5月15日，当Zuckerberg实现新的隐私更新时，这将在他的数据隐私的一系列破损承诺中只有一个。 2016年，WhatsApp在其术语和条件下实施了更新，该条款和条件允许与Facebook共享的用户的电话号码。用户在技术上进行了30天的通知以选择退出。然而，许多人没有意识到可能的选择退出并错过了他们可以这样做的小窗口，而从那以后加入的大约10亿用户根本没有选择。
 Whatsapp于1月宣布其最新的隐私更新，更改最初意味着在2月8日生效。然而，一个流行的露头将日期推回到5月15日，毫无疑问，希望公共愤怒褪色，为安静的实施铺平道路。 
但公共愤怒没有褪色。因此，Facebook已选择熟悉的策略：无论如何，通过新的政策变革播种和力量。公司正在播放WhatsApp用户接受5月15日或在新的不透明时间范围内的政策变化，几周额外几周。那些忽视或拒绝该决定的人将无法访问基本WhatsApp运行。
 时间目前正在为Zuckerberg运行在全球通信的最新攻击中，并保护所有WhatsApp用户的隐私，以便在这个关键时刻进行民主和世界各地的异议。
 因此，Facebook是由于争论其最新变更仅对这些隐私更新的意义展示了这些隐私更新的几个月，因此只会影响与业务账户的沟通（WhatsApp业务于2018年1月推出）。事实上，更改将允许Facebook从WhatsApp用户收取支付和交易数据，这意味着Facebook将能够使用更多的个性化广告来收集更多的数据和目标用户。 WhatsApp还在其隐私政策中删除了关于选择与Facebook共享数据的隐私政策。 Facebook认为，这简单地反映了自2016年以来已经到位的地方。这正是问题所在.Today的whatsapp与Facebook分享了大量信息，它承诺它不会，包括帐户信息，电话号码，人们多久和多久频率WhatsApp，有关如何与其他用户，IP地址，浏览器详细信息，语言，时区等互动的信息。此最新疫苗已突出显示多年的数据共享多年来，没有大多数用户的知识。
 Facebook再次滥用其垄断位置并计数缺乏选择，以确保它可以从WhatsApp中提取更多的财富。而这一次没有选择。作为由48个国家和美国地区针对Facebook的48个国家和地区的诉讼的一部分，大部分是由于其收购Instagram和Whatsapp，Letitia james的纽约律师们所说：“而不是竞争幻想，Facebook用它的权力来压制竞争，因此它可以利用用户并通过将个人数据转换为现金牛来赚取数十亿。“
 虽然，这不是欧洲人的案例，其中什么也非常受欢迎。由于欧洲联盟的一般数据保护监管，欧洲人的隐私受到保护。同时，在印度，超过4亿人使用Whatsapp，德里高等法院正在向Facebook提出退出选择。高等法院的恳求只是一个要求WhatsApp为其印度用户提供相同的隐私标准，因为它为欧盟提供的用户提供了相同的用户。世界各地的政策制定者有责任遵循诉讼，保护公民免受歧视发展中国家用户的侵害。
 我们不能让Mark Zuckerberg使用世界上最重要的消息服务之一作为他的最新数据Goldmine。 5月15日快速接近。我们必须确保为所有人提供隐私权。我们可以通过将停止留到Facebook的最新举措来开始对阵WhatsApp用户。但是Facebook再次表明，当涉及到数据剥削时，没有承诺它不会破坏。鉴于Facebook的商业模式，预测隐私入侵和消费者剥削，Facebook不应被允许获得WhatsApp。打破Facebook的时间已经到来。行动的时间现在。
 Burcu Kilic指示公共公民的数字权利计划。索菲亚Crabbe-Field是民主的助理编辑：思想杂志
note_en:
Facebook is pushing a mysterious and aggressive ‘privacy update’ on WhatsApp users. Here’s why
   I t’s the messaging app that connects a  quarter of the world’s population, but many Americans still have haven’t heard of WhatsApp. That’s because most phone plans in the United States  provide a standard flat rate for texting that allows people to communicate freely within the country. But throughout much of the world, including many of the world’s poorest countries,  people are charged for every single message they send and receive.
 That is why, since its launch in 2009, WhatsApp has become a vital resource for billions of people – and they are prepared to defend it. When the Lebanese government tried to bring in a “ WhatsApp tax”, charging $0.20 daily for calls made on the app, it helped trigger the mass protests that swept the country in 2019.
  One thing that does connect Americans to WhatsApp users, however, is Facebook chief executive Mark Zuckerberg and his flagrant disregard for data privacy. Facebook  acquired WhatsApp in 2014 in a move to consolidate control over global communications. Now Zuckerberg is moving ahead with a change to WhatsApp’s privacy policy that aims to commercialize our communications in order to feed Facebook’s insatiable greed.
 At the time of WhatsApp’s purchase in 2014, the app did not collect phone numbers, metadata, or other contact information. Facebook promised to keep it this way. “We are absolutely not going to change plans around WhatsApp and the way it uses user data,” Zuckerberg  claimed. “WhatsApp is going to operate completely autonomously.”
 Yet on 15 May, when Zuckerberg implements a new privacy update, this will be just one more in a series of his broken promises on data privacy. In 2016, WhatsApp implemented  an update to its terms and conditions that allowed data like a user’s phone number to be shared with Facebook. Users were technically given 30 days’ notice to opt out. However, many were unaware of the possible opt-out and missed the small window in which they could do so, while the approximately one billion users who joined since were given  no choice at all.
 WhatsApp announced its latest privacy update in January, with changes initially meant to  take effect on 8 February. However, a popular outcry pushed the date back to 15 May, with Facebook no doubt hoping that public outrage would fade, paving the way for a quiet implementation.
 But public outrage has not faded. And so Facebook has opted for a familiar tactic:  sow confusion and force through its new policy change anyway. The company is pestering WhatsApp users to accept the policy change by 15 May or, under a new opaque timeframe,  a few additional weeks. Those who ignore or refuse the decision will lose access to basic WhatsApp functioning.
 Time is now running for Zuckerberg to reverse course in this latest assault on global communications – and protect the privacy of all  WhatsApp users at this critical hour for democracy and dissent around the world.
 Facebook, for its part, has spent the months since the announcement downplaying the significance of these privacy updates by arguing that its latest changes  will only affect communication with business accounts (WhatsApp Business was launched in January 2018). In truth, the changes will  allow Facebook to collect payment and transaction data from WhatsApp users, meaning Facebook will be able to gather even more data and target users with ever more personalized ads. WhatsApp has also removed a passage in its privacy policy about opting out of sharing data with Facebook. Facebook argues that this simply reflects what’s  been in place since 2016. That is exactly the problem.Today’s WhatsApp  shares a great deal of information with Facebook it promised it wouldn’t, including account information, phone numbers, how often and how long people use WhatsApp, information about how they interact with other users, IP addresses, browser details, language, time zone, etc. This latest incursion has highlighted just how much data sharing has been going on for years without most users’ knowledge.
 Facebook is again abusing its monopoly position and counting on a lack of options to make sure it can extract even more wealth from WhatsApp. And this time there is no opting out. As part of an ongoing 2020 lawsuit filed by 48 states and districts of the United States against Facebook, in large part due to its acquisitions of Instagram and WhatsApp, New York attorney general Letitia James has  argued: “Instead of competing on the merits, Facebook used its powers to suppress competition so it could take advantage of users and make billions by converting personal data into a cash cow.”
 This isn’t the case for Europeans, though, among whom WhatsApp is also extremely popular. Europeans’ privacy  is protected thanks to the European Union’s General Data Protection Regulation. Meanwhile, in India, where  more than 400 million people use WhatsApp, the Delhi high court is  petitioning Facebook to provide an opt-out option. The plea in the high court is simply a request that WhatsApp provide the same privacy standards to its Indian users as it provides to those in the EU. Policymakers around the world have a duty to follow suit and protect citizens from encroachments that discriminate against users in the developing world.
 We cannot let Mark Zuckerberg’s use one of the world’s most important messaging services as his latest data goldmine. May 15 is fast approaching. We must ensure a right to privacy for all. We can begin by putting a stop to Facebook’s latest move against WhatsApp users. But Facebook has shown, once again, that when it comes to data exploitation there are no promises it won’t break. Given Facebook’s business model, predicated on privacy invasion and consumer exploitation, Facebook should never have been allowed to acquire WhatsApp. The time to break up Facebook has come. The time to act is now.
 Burcu Kilic directs Digital Rights Program at Public Citizen. Sophia Crabbe-Field is associate editor at Democracy: A Journal of Ideas
title: 担心与Facebook的信息有多少信息
title_en: Be worried about how much info WhatsApp shares with Facebook

经过8年的工作后，28,000名提交，7,400拉申请，4,100封闭问题 -  Scala 3终于出了。自2012年12月6日的第一次提交以来，超过一百人为该项目做出了贡献。今天，Scala 3纳入了型式理论的最新研究以及Scala 2的行业经验。我们已经看到了Scala中社区的良好（或不太好）的工作。根据这种经验，我们创造了第三次迭代Scala  - 易于使用，学习和规模。
  在Scala 3最终有很多新功能，你可能想知道你应该在哪里开始学习所有这些。学习Scala 3的进入地方是其文档。
 为了获得进入Scala 3的所有新功能，您可以看看Scala 3文章中的新功能。为了简化和更全面的介绍，请参阅Scala 3 Book。您可以在线尝试Scala 3，而无需通过SCASTIE在您的计算机上安装任何内容，或者您​​可以遵循“入门指南”将其安装在计算机上。
 与Scala 2相比，一件事完全在Scala 3中发生了变化：它是宏。您可以在专用文档中了解如何在Scala 3中工作。
 如果您是Scala 2的经验丰富的用户，您可以在Scala 2到Scala 3.您可能希望从Scala 2港口到Scala 3.您可能会发现迁移指南有用。它概述了Scala 2和Scala 3之间的兼容性故事：源兼容性，二进制兼容性，更改和丢弃功能，Metaprogramming。
 如果您在Scala旅程中需要任何帮助，则可以在此找到与Scala用户的各种资源。
  谁已经在Scala 3？您可以使用哪些库？一个回答这个问题的好地方是scaladex。 Scaladex是Scala库的索引，您可以通过语言版本，平台或库的作业类型来探索生态系统。在撰写本文时，Scala 3上有308个早期采用的库图书馆，而2597 Scala 2.13图书馆相比。

After 8 years of work, 28,000 commits, 7,400 pull requests, 4,100 closed issues – Scala 3 is finally out. Since the first commit on December 6th 2012, more than a hundred people have contributed to the project. Today, Scala 3 incorporates the latest research in type theory as well as the industry experience of Scala 2. We’ve seen what worked well (or not so well) for the community in Scala 2. Based on this experience we’ve created the third iteration of Scala – easy to use, learn, and scale.
  A lot of new features ended up in Scala 3, and you may wonder where you should start learning about all of it. The go-to place for learning Scala 3 is its  documentation.
 To get a taste of all the new features that went into Scala 3, you can have a look at the  New in Scala 3 article. For a streamlined and more thorough introduction, see the  Scala 3 Book. You can try Scala 3 online without installing anything on your machine via  Scastie, or you can follow the  Getting Started guide to install it on your machine.
 One thing has changed completely in Scala 3 compared to Scala 2: it is macros. You can learn more about how they work in Scala 3 in the dedicated  documentation.
 If you are a seasoned user of Scala 2, chances are you have some projects you’d like to port from Scala 2 to Scala 3. You may find the  Migration guide helpful. It outlines the compatibility story between Scala 2 and Scala 3: source compatibility, binary compatibility, changed and dropped features, metaprogramming.
 And if you need any help in your Scala journey, you can find various resources to talk to fellow Scala users  here.
  Who’s already on Scala 3? Which libraries can you already use? A great place to answer this question is  Scaladex. Scaladex is an index of Scala libraries where you can explore the ecosystem by language version, platform or the type of job a library does. At the time of writing this article, there are 308 early adopter libraries on Scala 3, compared to 2597 Scala 2.13 libraries.
  We intend to continue releasing every 6 weeks after 3.0.0, bumping the patch version every time we do so. A stable 3.0.x release will be preceded with 3.0.x-RC1 release candidate 6 weeks prior to such a stable release. Such patch releases will contain bug fixes for bugs affecting the respective minor version. Patch versions will be forward and backward compatible with each other over the source, binary and TASTy compatibilities.
 Of course, we intend to continue the development of the language in ways other than bug fixing. New language features and standard library APIs will appear in future minor versions. As any addition in library APIs, these may break backward source compatibility in minor, rare ways. However, minor releases will not break backward binary or TASTy compatibility. Concretely, that means that libraries built with Scala 3.0.0 will keep working with Scala 3.x.y!
  Scala 3 now has a set of widely-used community libraries that are built against every nightly Scala 3snapshot. At present, there are 67 projects included, and the number is growing rapidly.Join our  community buildto make sure that our regression suite includes your library.

一些产品，在后似乎似乎是他们立即注定成功。采取洗碗机，汽车或灯泡 - 每次都会为之前的产品创造了一个产品，除了较差的化身。一旦人们在方便，速度或可靠性方面知道这些重大升级，确保了成功和利润。
 在8月1日，MTV庆祝其发射40周年，鉴于渠道的全球品牌普遍存在及其历史，使任何人的星星从Cyndi Lauper发布到罗伯德德克，很容易想象通道有液体氢气 - Celebs的推动推动推出的发射，由Paparazzi拍摄，并被华尔街所在。
 但是MTV的创作不是常春藤联盟Boffins的社交网络故事，以追踪伟大;它更像是坏消息熊，这是一个陷入困境的失败者。 MTV的发射促使怀疑主义，如果不是漠不关心或嘲弄，来自音乐，广告和电视行业。在发布之前，只有几十人相信网络会成功，所有这些都在那里工作。
  MTV通常不会承认自己的周年纪念日，因为就像一个中年演员一样，它不想承认其年龄。但对于1960年至1980年间出生的很多人来说，40周年纪念日带回了荒谬的发型，紧张的氨纶和令人愤慨的奇观的令人愉快的回忆。 Mississippi格莱美博物馆甚至标志着展览的周年纪念日，“MTV变成四十：我仍然想要我的MTV，”开放5月14日，在2022年夏天继续，并包括麦当娜的衣服在“Vogue”视频中的纪念品迈克尔杰克逊的“肮脏的戴安娜”皮革套装，以及“每朵玫瑰的巨型声学吉他有它的荆棘。”
   MTV的初创员工大多是年轻人的不足，很少或没有电视体验。高级员工包括两个以后被誉为Visionaries的一个单眼高管，另一个在阿富汗和印度的进出口业务以外的另一个人以前没有任何运作。初级工作人员有责任，他们并没有近乎合格，告诉据说，并不知道据说限制了他们的规则。
  “我们是瘦，卑鄙，不知道我们所做的到底是什么，”汤姆·弗雷特在我想要我的MTV：2011年关于我用克拉格的书写的音乐视频革命的未经审查的故事。工作人员在曼哈顿中城的喜来登横渡的房间里闻起来，闻起来像Pastami，因为他们在卡内基熟食店之上。 “我的第一个办公室，”佩雷斯顿说，前进出口莫尔·佩雷尔·普罗姆，“是苏打储藏室的首席执行官。”
  MTV员工不得不穿过乔治华盛顿桥上的新泽西州乔治华盛顿大桥，看看午夜推出，因为曼哈顿没有有线运营商会同意携带MTV。网络在150到250个视频之间进行了某处 - 没有人确切地记得 - 并且每天都有24小时填写，因为他们没有广告商，频道别无选择，只能扮演一些更流行的视频（包括这样的非家庭名称作为训练营，鞋子，rupert hine，fischer-z，爵士吉他手lee ritenour和blotto每隔几个小时都在纽约州原住民外的乐队。如果你是4月葡萄酒的菲尔柯林斯的粉丝，这是一个很棒的消息，如果你是菲尔柯林斯的“在空中”或“在你和我之间”。存在超过250个视频，但几个记录标签，包括Polygram，MCA和Arista，拒绝将其中的副本送到MTV  - 免费赠送的点，以便免费赠送到快车上的网络的内容失败？ 
多年来，人们声称他们看到了MTV的第一个小时; Pat Benatar和Chynna Phillips都告诉我们他们看到了首次亮相的前几分钟。这可能是一个假的内存 -  MTV经常重新广播第一个小时，所以在过去的40年里，很多人都看到了它，因为网络的国家分配太糟糕了。在李堡，新泽西州堡，没有人看起来更有可能看。
 少数报告者努力努力解释MTV打印，因为短语音乐视频不存在。 “MTV编程中的主要成分是”视频记录“或”视频“：3分钟或4分钟的录像带所示的当前录像，”时间杂志表示，一个读取的句子，好像它通过谷歌翻译运行。
 MTV的商业模式是为其他人提供和融资的产品销售广告。那真是太棒了。但他们需要观众来使它工作。他们在第一年销售了500,000美元的广告，损失了约5000万美元，并有很大的努力要求更多。普通母公司普通士（美国快递与华纳通讯的合资企业）总裁兼首席执行官杰克·施耐德（American Contruct and Warner Communications）只给了他们200万美元，并明确了他的耐心。 MTV几乎是Kaput。
 这是200万美元进入AD Campaign Bob Pittman告诉同事是“我们的最后一次机会” -  Mick Jagger，Pete Townshend，David Bowie和其他明星出现在直接消费广告，吼叫，“呼叫您的电缆运营商和需求。你的MTV。“哪些孩子在这样的卷中表现出最稳定，最保守的电缆运营商勾结。
  这带来了观众，它导致了愿意徒步票据的纪录公司的广告和新的视频，因为MTV保证专辑销售的Airplay。网络的时机是迷人的; 1984年是流行音乐历史上最伟大的岁月之一，几乎每个粉碎都有视频。预算飙升（可卡因的线条物品通常被隐藏为手写的餐饮费用），生产质量有所改善，陈词滥调出现：宇宙飞船，摩托车，在砖墙巷道，疯狂的最大参考资料中张力，所以，如此多的矮人。视频传播了全国各地的性别，性别和眼睛构成的非动态思想，给同性恋，跨境或艺术学校青少年，他们没有看到自己代表在CNN或CBS上。
  但是那些值得将MTV从稳定业务转向主导地位的人是一个不适合网络格式的歌手：迈克尔·杰克逊。
 几十年后，MTV高管仍由Rick James，David Bowie等人的指控，以及他们的格式是种族主义的指责。他们坚持认为，一旦他们看着杰克逊的梦幻般的“Billie Jean”，他们将他添加到了格式。杰克逊的团队说，MTV拒绝了视频，只有在他的记录标签威胁到所有其他视频之后才会播放它。一个事实很清楚：这首歌已经出了两个月，在MTV开始播放视频之前，在广告牌图表上达到了第一款。 
几个星期，“Billie Jean”和“击败它”都沉重旋转。然后来“惊悚片”，最精心的（和昂贵的）视频。 MTV每小时发挥它，每次重复，评分飙升。 “迈克尔·杰克逊是MTV从大到巨大的原因，”约翰斯克斯是一家网络的创始高管告诉我们。
 单一型材垂死，通过有线电视的爆炸破裂。 1983年2月的M * A * S * H的决赛，它吸引了超过1.06亿观众，是网络电视持有美国的最后一个喘息。但在减少受众的景观中，MTV是独一无二的：很大程度上归人对迈克尔杰克逊，它成为奇点。每个人都在观看，并通过记录的业务，好莱坞，广告和时尚迅速传播。一切都变得了MTV-IZED。
 业务模式无法生存：录制标签开始怨恨提供MTV免费内容，竞争网络和视频节目出现，而MTV发现依靠Michael Jackson（或麦当娜或梵顿或任何人），以使巨大的视频在评级中创造了巨大的视频当那些艺术家没有新的单打。全视频格式经常推迟观众; Bon Jovi Fan会坐在运行DMC视频中，反之亦然吗？ MTV决定他们需要他们的编程是贴上的，这意味着实际显示，而不仅仅是视频：哟！ MTV Raps，游戏展示遥控器，与现实表演的成功在于MTV的黄金时代，现实世界取得了成功。如果MTV没有发明现实电视，那么它当然将其推入主流，网络快速更换了现实展示的视频。我们没有Cyndi Lauper，我们得到了Snooki;迈克尔杰克逊被奥巴府所取代。到了90年代初，MTV的效果到目前为止，彻底遍布，这不再是独一无二的。
  Tiktok再现MTV让其他人创造他们的编程，奖励类似的快速视觉能量，就像MTV一样，通常被解雇为一个不对的音乐创造力。记录标签法庭Tiktok，因为他们在80年代中期做了MTV，尽管他们似乎已经了解到了他们的课程，现在为内容收费。这些天你不能打破一个新的艺术家，无论是流行音乐，说唱还是国家，没有Tiktok支持。
 去年，雅虎新闻标题大声喊道，“Tiktok是新的MTV，是我们都上船上的时候了。” Billie Eilish和Tiktok为彼此做了迈克尔·杰克逊和MTV曾经做过的事情。并且有一个更大，变革的相似性：网络三十年来第一次标志着，一个新的消费产品成为摇滚明星，这是一个现在普遍的发展。 Apple已售出超过4亿个iPod和近20亿个iPhone，其中矮人是披头士乐队的18300万Riaa认证的专辑销售，摇滚乐和＃39;最受欢迎的乐队。
 但是，在不同的时候，Pundits已经加冕了Myspace，ESPN，Roblox，摇滚乐队，葡萄，有趣或死亡，以及Friendster作为“新的MTV”，看看它是如何解决的。 Bob Pittman现在运行媒体帝国专注于甚至比音乐视频更旧的技术：地面无线电。即使是Facebook，凭借数十亿用户，可能会牺牲政府监管或反垄断诉讼。
 作为一个聪明人曾经提醒我们，视频杀死了无线电星星。无论是多么受欢迎，没有培养基稳定或永久。广播无处不在，直到电视发布。 FM收音机是臀部，直到出现卫星收音机。 CD替换乙烯基，然后流替换CD，然后相册开始卷土重来。 Snooki和Gang正在返回MTV，这显然是在6月份的新系列泽西海岸家庭度假的想法。这些日子的流行介质的半衰期短。 
当然，MTV不是“新的MTV”。维亚康姆（1985年购买MTV的媒体公司）未能转向互联网时代并失去对Zeitgeist的控制。该频道现在视觉平淡，似乎扮演了一个无穷无尽的荒谬剧集。几年前，汤姆弗雷斯顿说：“它打破了我的心”来看看现行的MTV状态。
  没有什么可以是“新的MTV”，因为2021与1981年不同，因为1981年从1901年起，如果有一个新的奇点，这是互联网本身，足以让所有人都足够大的腹部。大众媒体已被利基媒体所取代，以及像大多数流行文化一样的音乐平台都分层。只有技术界，就像在1981年开始的10年期间一样。
   梳妆台上的所有产品均独立地由我们的编辑独立选择。但是，当您通过我们的零售链接购买某些东西时，我们可能会获得联盟委员会。
   - 一个浅谈伊丽莎白二世伊丽莎白二世的贴心观点 - 袋子发射了oxycontin。每个人现在都知道它。 - 独家摘录：世界底层的冰冷的死亡 -  Lolita，Blake Bailey和Me  -  Kate Middleton和君主制的未来 - 偶尔在数字时代约会的恐怖恐怖 - 这是一个健康，平衡的13个最佳面部油皮肤 - 来自档案：火种和“约会启示录”的曙光 - 注册“皇家钟表”通讯从肯辛顿宫内和超越的所有喋喋不休。
note_en:
Some products, in hindsight, seem like they were immediately destined for success. Take dishwashers, automobiles, or light bulbs—each created a demand for a product that hadn’t existed before, except in inferior incarnations. Once people knew about these significant upgrades in convenience, speed, or reliability, success and profit was assured.
 On August 1, MTV celebrates the 40th anniversary of its launch, and given the channel’s worldwide brand ubiquity and its history of making stars of anyone from to  Cyndi Lauper to  Puck to  Rob Dyrdek, it’s easy to imagine the channel had a liquid-hydrogen-fueled launch attended by celebs, photographed by paparazzi, and hailed by Wall Street.
 But MTV’s creation isn’t a  Social Network tale of Ivy League boffins on track for greatness; it’s more like  The Bad News Bears, a tale of scrappy underdogs. MTV’s launch prompted skepticism, if not indifference or mockery, from the music, advertising, and television industries. Prior to the launch, only a few dozen people believed the network would succeed, and all of them worked there.
  MTV usually doesn’t acknowledge its own anniversaries, because like a middle-aged actor, it doesn’t want to admit its age. But for lots of people born between 1960 and 1980, a 40th anniversary brings back delightful memories of ridiculous haircuts, tight spandex, and outrageous spectacle. The Grammy Museum Mississippi is even marking the anniversary with an exhibit, “MTV Turns Forty: I Still Want My MTV,” that opens May 14, continues through the summer of 2022, and includes  such mementos as  Madonna’s dress in the “Vogue” video, Michael Jackson’s “Dirty Diana” leather suit, and  Bret Michaels’s acoustic guitar from “Every Rose Has Its Thorn.”
   MTV’s startup staff were mostly young misfits with little or no TV experience. The senior staff included two one-eyed executives who were later hailed as visionaries, and another who’d previously run nothing except an import-export business in Afghanistan and India. The junior staff were given responsibilities they weren’t nearly qualified for, told to figure it out, and succeeded by not knowing the rules that supposedly constrained them.
  “We were lean and mean and didn’t know what the hell we were doing,”  Tom Freston said in   I Want My MTV: The Uncensored Story of the Music Video Revolution, a 2011 book I wrote with  Craig Marks. The staff worked out of cramped rooms at the Sheraton in midtown Manhattan, which smelled like pastrami, because they were above the Carnegie Deli. “My first office,” said Freston, the former import-export mogul who would go on to become the CEO of Viacom, “was a soda storeroom.”
  MTV employees had to shlep across the George Washington Bridge to Fort Lee, New Jersey, to see the midnight launch, because no cable operator in Manhattan would agree to carry MTV. The network had somewhere between 150 and 250 videos—no one remembers exactly—and with 24 hours to fill every day because they had no advertisers, the channel had no choice but to play a few of the more popular videos (including such non-household names as Bootcamp, Shoes, Rupert Hine, Fischer-Z, jazz guitarist  Lee Ritenour, and Blotto, a band who were all but unknown outside their native Albany, New York) every few hours. This was great news if you were a fan of  Phil Collins’s “In the Air Tonight” or “Just Between You and Me” by April Wine. More than 250 videos existed, but several record labels, including PolyGram, MCA, and Arista, refused to give copies of them to MTV—what was the point of giving away, for free, content for a network that was on a fast train to failure?
  Over the years, people have claimed they saw the first hour of MTV;  Pat Benatar and  Chynna Phillips both told us they’d seen the first few minutes of the debut. This is likely a false memory—MTV has frequently rebroadcast the first hour, so lots of people have seen it in the last 40 years, as a repeat—because the network’s national distribution was so bad. It’s much more likely that outside of Fort Lee, New Jersey, no one at all was watching.
 The few reporters who paid attention struggled to explain MTV in print, because the phrase  music video didn’t exist. “The main ingredients in MTV’s programming are ‘video records’ or ‘videos’: current recordings illustrated by 3- or 4-minute videotapes,”  Time magazine said, a sentence that reads as though it was run through Google Translate.
 MTV’s business model was to sell advertising for a product someone else made and financed. It was brilliant. But they needed viewers to make it work. They sold only $500,000 of advertising in their first year, lost about $50 million, and had the temerity to ask for a lot more. Jack Schneider, the president and CEO of parent company WASEC (a joint venture between American Express and Warner Communications), gave them only $2 million, and made it clear he was running out of patience. MTV was nearly kaput.
 That $2 million went into an ad campaign  Bob Pittman told colleagues was “our last chance”— Mick Jagger, Pete Townshend, David Bowie, and other stars appeared in a direct-to-consumer ad, bellowing, “Call your cable operator and  demand your MTV.” Which kids did in such volume that even the stoniest, most conservative cable operators buckled.
  That brought in viewers, which led to more advertising and fresh videos from record companies who were willing to foot the bill because airplay on MTV guaranteed album sales. The network’s timing was charmed; 1984 was one of the greatest years in pop-music history, and nearly every smash had a video. Budgets skyrocketed (the line item for cocaine was usually hidden as a handwritten catering bill), production quality improved, and clichés emerged: spaceships, motorcycles, bands posturing in brick-wall alleyways, Mad Max references, and so, so, so many dwarves. Videos spread nonnormative ideas about sex, gender, and eye makeup across the country, giving solace to gay, trans, or art school teens who didn’t see themselves represented on CNN or CBS.
  But the person who deserves the most credit for turning MTV from a stable business to a dominant one is a singer who didn’t fit the network’s format: Michael Jackson.
 Decades later, MTV executives are still rankled by accusations from Rick James, David Bowie, and others that their format was racist. They insist that as soon as they looked at Jackson’s fantastic video for “Billie Jean,” they added him to the format. Jackson’s team says MTV rejected the video and played it only after  his record label threatened to pull all their other videos. One fact is clear: The song had been out for two months and had reached number one on the Billboard charts before MTV began playing the video.
  For several weeks, “Billie Jean” and “Beat It” were both in heavy rotation. Then came “Thriller,” the most elaborate (and expensive) video ever made. MTV played it every hour, and with each repeat, ratings spiked. “Michael Jackson was the reason MTV went from big to huge,”  John Sykes, one of the network’s founding executives, told us.
 The monoculture was dying, fractured by an explosion in cable TV. The February 1983 finale of  M*A*S*H, which drew more than  106 million viewers, was the last gasp of network TV’s hold on America. But in a landscape of reduced audiences, MTV was unique: Thanks largely to Michael Jackson, it became the singularity. Everyone watched, and the ramifications spread quickly through the record business, Hollywood, advertising, and fashion. Everything became MTV-ized.
 The business model couldn’t survive: Record labels began to resent giving MTV free content, competing networks and video shows emerged, and MTV discovered that relying on Michael Jackson (or Madonna or Van Halen or whomever) to make great videos created volatility in ratings when those artists didn’t have new singles. The all-video format often put off viewers; would a Bon Jovi fan sit through a Run-DMC video, and vice versa? MTV decided they needed their programming to be stickier, which meant actual shows, not just videos:  Yo! MTV Raps, the game show  Remote Control, and the reality show whose success brought down the curtain on the golden age of MTV,  The Real World. If MTV didn’t invent reality TV, it certainly pushed it into the mainstream, and the network quickly replaced videos with reality shows. Instead of Cyndi Lauper, we got  Snooki; Michael Jackson was replaced by the Osbournes. By the early ’90s, MTV’s effect had spread so far and so thoroughly that it was no longer unique.
  TikTok reproduces MTV’s model of letting others create their programming, rewards a similar quick-cut visual energy, and like MTV, is often dismissed as a fad that’s bad for music creativity. Record labels court TikTok, as they did MTV in the mid-’80s, though they do seem to have learned their lesson, and now charge for the content. You can’t break a new artist these days, whether pop, rap, or country, without TikTok support.
 Last year,  a Yahoo news headline shouted, “TikTok Is the New MTV, and It’s Time We All Get on Board.”  Billie Eilish and TikTok did for each other what Michael Jackson and MTV once did. And there’s a larger, transformative similarity: The network marked the first time in decades that a new consumer product became a rock star, a development that is now common. Apple has sold  more than 400 million iPods and  almost 2 billion iPhones, which dwarfs the 183 million RIAA-certified albums sales of the Beatles, rock's most popular band.
 But at various times, pundits have crowned MySpace, ESPN, Roblox,  Rock Band, Vine, Funny or Die, and Friendster as “the new MTV,” and look at how that worked out. Bob Pittman now runs a media empire focused on a technology even older than music videos: terrestrial radio. Even Facebook, with its billions of users, could fall prey to government regulation or anti-monopoly lawsuits.
 As a wise man once reminded us, video killed the radio star. No matter how popular it is, no medium is stable or permanent. Radio was ubiquitous until TV came along. FM radio was hip until satellite radio emerged. CDs replaced vinyl, then streaming replaced CDs, then albums started to make a comeback. Snooki and the gang are returning to MTV, which is evidently out of ideas, for a new series of   Jersey Shore Family Vacation in June. The half-life of a popular medium is shorter than ever these days.
 And certainly, MTV isn’t “the new MTV.” Viacom (the media company that bought MTV in 1985) failed to transition to the internet era and lost control of the zeitgeist. The channel, now visually bland, seems to play an endless loop of  Ridiculousness episodes. A few years ago,  Tom Freston said “It breaks my heart” to look at the current state of MTV.
  Nothing can be “the new MTV,” because 2021 is as different from 1981 as 1981 was from 1901. If there is a new singularity, it’s the Internet itself, a rabbit hole big enough for all mankind. Mass media has been replaced by niche media, and music platforms, like most of pop culture, are stratified. Only technology unites us, much as it did in a 10-year period beginning in 1981.

title: 当MTV在40年前借鉴时，每个人都认为它会失败
title_en: When MTV debuted 40 years ago, everyone thought it would fail

Facebook Inc.丢失了一个竞标，以阻止欧盟隐私决定，可以暂停其向美国中的计算机服务器发送有关欧洲用户数据的能力，打开往预先设置其数据流中断的路径。
  爱尔兰的高等法院周五驳回了Facebook的所有程序投诉关于它在八月收到的数据流量的初步决定，从爱尔兰的数据保护委员会收到。它拒绝了Facebook的声明，监管机构给出了太少的时间来回应或过早地判断。
  法院在9月份待定的初步决定待决定，如果最终确定，可以暂停社会媒体公司将有关欧盟用户的个人信息暂停到Facebook的美国的服务器中。
  为了遵守，Facebook可能必须重新设计它的服务，其中大多数它从欧洲用户收集的数据，或者至少暂时停止完全服务于它们。在今年的证券申请中，Facebook表示，将监管机构的决定应用于“实质性地影响我们的业务，财务状况和业务结果”。
  虽然周五的法院决定是一个程序之一，但潜在的问题是跨大西洋贸易和数字经济的核心。法律专家表示，爱尔兰临时秩序的逻辑可以向其他受美国的其他大型技术公司申请监督法律，如云服务和电子邮件提供商 - 可能导致跨大西洋数据流普遍讨论跨大西洋数据流动。
note_en:
Facebook Inc. lost a bid to block a European Union privacy decision that could suspend its ability to send data about European users to computer servers in the U.S., opening a pathway toward a precedent-setting interruption of its data flows.
  Ireland’s High Court dismissed Friday all of Facebook’s procedural complaints about a preliminary decision on data flows that it received in August from Ireland’s Data Protection Commission. It rejected Facebook’s claims that the regulator had given it too little time to respond or issued a judgment prematurely.
  The preliminary decision, which the court stayed in September pending its decision, could, if finalized, force the social-media company to suspend sending personal information about EU users to Facebook’s servers in the U.S.
  To comply, Facebook would likely have to re-engineer its service to silo off most data it collects from European users, or stop serving them entirely, at least temporarily. In a securities filing this year, Facebook said that applying the regulator’s decision would “materially and adversely affect our business, financial condition and results of operations.”
  While Friday’s court decision is a procedural one, the underlying questions are central to trans-Atlantic trade and the digital economy. Legal experts say the logic in Ireland’s provisional order could apply to other large tech companies that are subject to U.S. surveillance laws, such as cloud services and email providers—potentially leading to widespread disruption of trans-Atlantic data flows.
title: Facebook丢失了衡量欧盟 - 美国的统治。 数据流量
title_en: Facebook Loses Bid to Block Ruling on EU-U.S. Data Flows

WASHINGTON — A detainee at Guantánamo Bay has agreed to a deal intended to lead to his release in the next few years in return for giving up the right to question the C.I.A. in court about its torture program, United States government officials said.
 The deal, negotiated by the Pentagon official who oversees the military commissions that serve as a court for some detainees, was reached in recent weeks, and comes as a number of those who have been charged at Guantánamo are seeking to cite their abuse at the hands of the C.I.A. as part of their defense.
 Under the deal, the prisoner, Majid Khan, 41, who has pleaded guilty to serving as a courier for Al Qaeda, would complete his prison sentence as early as next year and no later than 2025 and then could be released to another country, assuming one will take him, according to people who have seen the terms or are familiar with its details.
 In exchange, Mr. Khan will not use his sentencing proceedings to invoke a  landmark war court decision that allowed him to call witnesses from the C.I.A.’s secret prison network to testify about his torture.
 The arrangement means that the C.I.A. for now will avoid a further accounting in court for its use of what it called “enhanced interrogation techniques” under the Bush administration after the attacks of Sept. 11, 2001.
 Mr. Khan, a citizen of Pakistan who went to high school in Maryland, was captured in Pakistan in 2003 and held incommunicado by the C.I.A. for three years. He was kept in darkness for years and in his second year of detention, the agency “infused” a purée of pasta, sauce, nuts, raisins and hummus into his rectum because he went on a hunger strike, according to a  2014 Senate investigation. He was also sleep deprived, kept naked and hung by his wrists, and hooded, to the point of hallucinations.
 Mr. Khan was transferred to Guantánamo Bay in 2006 and saw a lawyer for the first time in his fourth year of detention. In 2012,  he pleaded guilty to terrorism-related charges stemming from his work for Al Qaeda after the Sept. 11 attacks, and agreed to postpone his sentencing while he cooperated with government prosecutors.
 On April 16, he and his lawyers reached agreement with the overseer of military commissions for a sentence that would end sometime between early next year and March 1, 2025.
 The agreement itself is under seal, at least until a judge questions Mr. Khan on whether he voluntarily entered into it. But several people, speaking on the condition of anonymity to describe details of the deal, said that it has a sentencing range of 11 to 14 years, applied starting with his guilty plea in 2012.
 The jury in the case will still be instructed to impose a sentence of 25 to 40 years. But under the deal, the judge will then reduce the sentence to the agreed 11 to 14 years, with the precise length depending on a number of factors including the government’s assessment of his cooperation with U.S. authorities.
  Mr. Khan’s lawyers declined to comment. The C.I.A. referred questions about the deal to the convening authority, the overseer of the court at Guantánamo. A spokeswoman for the agency said the   “C.I.A.’s detention and interrogation program ended in 2009.”
 The deal averted a looming showdown between prosecutors and the judge in Mr. Khan’s case, Col. Douglas K. Watkins of the Army, over the judge’s order to take C.I.A. witnesses to Guantánamo Bay to testify about Mr. Khan’s torture during his 2003-06 detention in the overseas C.I.A. prison system, known as black sites.
 Colonel Watkins ruled last year that a military commissions judge had the power to award Mr. Khan time off his sentence for “pretrial punishment.” He ordered a fact-finding hearing at Guantánamo on Mr. Khan’s claims and said, if true, his descriptions of his treatment amounted to torture and pretrial punishment.
  Separately, Colonel Watkins awarded him a year off his eventual sentence because  prosecutors failed to disclose certain evidence. Colonel Watkins retires from the Army on Aug. 1 and was replaced on the case Wednesday by an Air Force judge,  Col. Mark W. Milam.
 The agreement is the first involving a Guantánamo detainee that the Biden administration has reached since taking office. It was made by Jeffrey D. Wood, a National Guard colonel who was appointed by the Trump administration to the civilian role of convening authority for military commissions.
 The timetable for Mr. Khan’s release gives the Biden administration time to restart negotiations with other governments, suspended during the Trump administration, to send detainees no longer deemed a national security threat from Guantánamo Bay to countries that commit to keep an eye on them and, typically, restrict their travel.
  Prosecutors had repeatedly argued that the military judge had no power to hold a hearing on a Guantánamo prisoner’s mistreatment and then reduce a sentence. They lost, though some government officials continue to argue that the ruling could be reversed.
 The prosecutors then resisted having witnesses testify at Guantánamo Bay, even anonymously, about the torture of Mr. Khan. A U.S. government official said there were questions about whether specific witnesses the judge had ordered to testify  had actually seen Mr. Khan in C.I.A. detention.
 The issue had been simmering but had not come to a head because travel restrictions during the coronavirus pandemic brought most military commission hearings to a standstill for the last year.
 The question of whether Mr. Khan could receive a reduction in his sentence because of his torture was also a potential model for the defense in the  capital conspiracy case against Khalid Shaikh Mohammed and four other men accused of plotting the Sept. 11 attacks. Defense lawyers for all five defendants say there is evidence that each was systematically tortured in the black sites, and they want a judge or jury to hear graphic details about it to avert a death sentence when  the long-delayed case eventually proceeds.
 Two contract psychologists who devised the C.I.A.’s interrogation program,  James Mitchell and John Bruce Jessen, have been publicly identified. But the identities of the people who interrogated Mr. Khan, and in which countries where they did it, are still classified at the court, which operates under rules that the government says are intended to balance state secrets and fair trial rights.
 Prosecutors argued that anonymous, in-person testimony about Mr. Khan’s treatment, whether in a classified session or in public, risked exposing covert U.S. government employees, and said it was not possible to take them to Guantánamo Bay. That left the possibility of the judge ordering their appearances, prosecutors refusing to bring them and as a remedy, the judge reducing Mr. Khan’s sentence.
 Government officials with knowledge of the deal said that the C.I.A. wanted to avoid having its officers exposed, and did not want them to testify at Guantánamo, but that Mr. Wood in his capacity as convening authority decided on his own to defuse the dispute over the witnesses.
 Descriptions of the agreement suggest there are victories for both sides. When the court finally assembles a sentencing jury at Guantánamo, Mr. Khan will be permitted to describe his torture for the panel of military officers that will sentence him — although perhaps not in open court. He just cannot call corroborating witnesses.
 In exchange, according to a  filing on April 22, Mr. Khan’s lawyers will also ask the judge after sentencing to void the June 2020 ruling that found credit for pretrial punishment is an available remedy at a military commission — undercutting its potential use in the Sept. 11 case.
  Mr. Khan has been kept apart from the other former C.I.A. prisoners at Guantánamo since he pleaded guilty. At that time, he became a government informant, and has been debriefed on demand although prosecutors have yet to hold a trial where his testimony would be needed.
 In pleading guilty he admitted to delivering $50,000 from Mr. Mohammed to militants in Indonesia that was used to finance  the bombing of a Marriott hotel in Jakarta, Indonesia, in 2003, killing 11 people. Three men at Guantánamo have been charged in that plot, but  have yet to be arraigned and have no trial date.
 During the Trump administration, Mr. Khan was also listed as a government witness in a planned federal prosecution of another Pakistani man, Uzair Paracha. Mr. Paracha was convicted in 2005 in New York of federal terrorism-related offenses, but the conviction was overturned. Rather than retry him last year,  federal prosecutors dropped the case in exchange for Mr. Paracha voluntarily giving up his U.S. residency and returning to Pakistan, after 17 years of incarceration.
 For Mr. Khan, the path out of Guantánamo may be more complex. Successive U.S. administrations have argued that a convicted war criminal who completes his sentence may still be held at Guantánamo in the quasi-prisoner of war status of a detainee, as long as the United States considers itself to be at war with Al Qaeda and other terrorist groups.
 Also, it is unclear where Mr. Khan would go. He was born in Saudi Arabia, lived as a child in Pakistan but went to high school in suburban Baltimore and had asylum in the United States before he returned to Pakistan after the Sept. 11 attacks. By law, he cannot be sent to the United States.

 该研究，在4月份出版的英国的智库战略对话研究所，一名基于英国的智库研究极端主义，分析了亚马逊的书籍推荐算法，了解关于白色民族主义者和最基单的内容，冠状病毒否认，冠状病毒否认，以及关于Qanon和欺诈在2020年的阴谋美国总统大选。
 该分析发现，该平台的推荐算法，这些算法，它建议浏览平台上的书籍的人员和作者 - 而对于大多数用户来说相对无害的，可以创建一个令人不安的管道进入极端主义文学，指导客户对阴谋理论和白色民族的文学。
 该研究发现亚马逊的算法建议“可以作为一个门户进入更广泛的阴谋理论和错误信息，或越来越激进的远方和白人民族主义内容。”
 Amazon的推荐算法通过告诉他们购买并观看的书籍的其他客户来说，向用户提出书籍。该算法根据ISD，创建反馈循环。点击一本关于阴谋的人是对同一和其他阴谋的提出的建议，包括关于Qanon，Debunned索赔关于美国的疫苗和欺诈的书籍的书籍。
 该算法的血统令牌可以将您带到锤子的书页是政变的关键：奥巴马，布伦南，梆子和中央情报局如何在总统特朗普，一般弗莱恩和其他人那里窥视明白的选举欺诈涉及2020年美国总统大选的欺诈。该页面建议与Illuminati，Rothschilds，“浮雕，”Pizzagate和外星人的书籍的链接。
 “我对建议书的怪异的聚宝盆感到震惊，”报告的作者和ISD分析师的作者埃莉斯托马斯·埃尔斯·托马斯（Elise Thomas）告诉我。她特别令人震惊地看到平台上的书籍，由九个角度（O9A），一个基于英国的纳粹撒旦集团的秩序，这已与许多恐怖犯罪联系起来，提示呼吁将其视为恐怖组织。 
“这些信念非常极端，我真的很糟糕地看到他们只是坐在亚马逊上，”她说。 “由于进行这项研究，我了解了我以前没有知道的其他09A文本，因为亚马逊的建议。我认为这是这里潜在伤害的一个例子。“
 其他例子将读者指向极端主义文献比比皆是。点击书籍白度：原始罪，建议包括关于欧洲ethnostates的标题，“白人身份政法”和新的世界秩序阴谋。
 亚马逊最近删除了1978年的White Supremacist小说的特纳日记，但是一本搜索本书现在将直接指导人们向AnaChist的食谱，一个由威廉·鲍威尔发表的炸弹制作指南，这些指南由威廉·鲍威尔发表的威廉·鲍威尔（Columbine）涉及哥伦比特的行为。和俄克拉荷马城市轰炸。
  Amazon Spokesperson表示，该公司认真对待战略对话所的担忧，并致力于为客户提供积极的经验。类似于销售书籍的其他商店，我们为客户提供了各种观点的访问，我们的购物和发现工具不设计为以特定的观点生成结果。“
 虽然任何关于禁止书籍的对话是复杂的，但托马斯表示，亚马逊的极端主义书籍算法的大修可以提供一个解决方案。这将涉及关闭书籍的自动推荐，促进阴谋理论，虚假信息或极端主义视角。
 “书籍仍在平台上，如果您搜索它们，可以找到它们，但如果您搜索一个并转到该页面，则不会向其他人推荐其他人，”她说。

A recently released report highlights how Amazon’s book recommendation algorithms can lead people to literature about extremism, white nationalism, and conspiracy theories, including QAnon and Covid-19 disinformation.
 The  study, published in April by the Institute for Strategic Dialogue, a UK-based think tank researching extremism, analyzed Amazon’s book recommendation algorithms for literature about white nationalist and far-right content, coronavirus denialism, and conspiracies about QAnon and fraud in the 2020 U.S. presidential election.
 The analysis found that the platform’s recommendation algorithms, which suggest titles and authors to people browsing through books on the platform — while relatively harmless for most users — can create a disturbing pipeline into extremist literature, directing customers toward conspiracy theories and white nationalist literature.
 The study found Amazon’s algorithmic recommendations “could serve as a gateway into a broader universe of conspiracy theories and misinformation, or to increasingly radical far-right and white nationalist content.”
 Amazon’s recommendation algorithm suggests books to users by telling them what other customers who clicked on the book bought and viewed. This algorithm, according to the ISD, creates a feedback loop. People who click on a book about a conspiracy are fed recommendations for the same and other conspiracies, including books about QAnon, debunked claims about vaccines and fraud in the U.S. 2020 presidential election.
 A descent into this algorithmic dystopia could take you to the book  page for  The Hammer is the Key to the Coup:  How Obama, Brennan, Clapper, and the CIA spied on President Trump, General Flynn … and everyone  else, written by the  proponents of a debunked election fraud conspiracy about the 2020 U.S. presidential election. The page suggests links to books about the Illuminati, the Rothschilds, the “Scamdemic,” Pizzagate, and aliens.
 “I was shocked by the cornucopia of weirdness in the recommendations,” Elise Thomas, the author of the report and an ISD analyst, told me. She was especially alarmed to see books on the platform by the founder of the Order of Nine Angles (O9A),  a UK-based Nazi satanic group that has been tied to a number of terror offences, prompting calls to outlaw it as a terrorist organization.
 “These beliefs are very extreme, and I was genuinely quite taken aback to see them just sitting on Amazon,” she said. “As a result of doing this research, I learned about other 09A texts I didn’t previously know about because of Amazon’s recommendations. I think that’s an example of the potential harm here.”
 Other examples directing readers to extremist literature abound. Click on the book   Whiteness: The Original Sin, and recommendations include titles about European ethnostates, “white identity politics,” and New World Order conspiracies.
 Amazon recently removed the 1978 white supremacist  novel  The Turner Diaries, but a search for the book will now direct people to  The Anarchist’s Cookbook, a bomb-making guide published in 1971 by William Powell that has been  linked to acts of violence including the Columbine shooting and the Oklahoma City bombing.
  An Amazon spokesperson said the company takes “concerns from the Institute for Strategic Dialogue seriously and are committed to providing a positive experience for our customers. Similar to other stores that sell books, we provide our customers with access to a variety of viewpoints and our shopping and discovery tools are not designed to generate results oriented to a specific point of view.”
 While any conversation about banning books is complex, Thomas says an overhaul of Amazon’s algorithm for extremist books could offer one solution. This would involve turning off the automatic recommendation of books promoting conspiracy theories, disinformation, or extremist views.
 “The books are still on the platform, you can find them if you search for them, but if you search for one and go to that page it’s not going to recommend you 20 others,” she said.
 The ISD report marks the most recent investigation into Amazon’s search algorithms. Previous analyses have also examined the role played by the digital giant’s search algorithms in leading customers into conspiracies and misinformation. A January 2021  study by researchers at the University of Washington, for example, revealed that nearly 10.5% of searches involving the term “vaccine” promote books containing anti-vaccine conspiracies and health misinformation.
    The story you just read is a small piece of a complex and an ever-changing storyline we are following as part of our coverage. These overarching storylines — whether the disinformation campaigns that are feeding the war on truth or the new technologies strengthening the growing authoritarianism, are the crises that Coda covers relentlessly and with singular focus. But we can’t do it without your help.    Support journalism that stays on the story. Coda Story is a 501(c)3 U.S non-profit. Your contribution to Coda Story is tax deductible.

note_en:
This drove a 20% boost in gaming sales globally and created nearly 20,000 jobs in 2020 alone. And it’s not expected to stop any time soon: According to research company  IBISWorld, the industry is set to grow again in 2021, adding to the year-over-year growth the industry has seen in the preceding half-decade.
 This is great news for the growing gaming industry and especially those looking to score a job at a company developing the next blockbuster. The unemployment rate is  already near zero for those with gaming development and design skills, which means there is an unprecedented opportunity to join the field.
   Gamesmith, a digital community dedicated to the gaming industry, currently has more than 5,750 open jobs posted on its site, with roles in design, engineering and animation leading the way. In other words, if you never considered a career in the gaming industry — or thought that your skill set wouldn’t translate — this expanding job market needs employees from all types of backgrounds. Chances are one of your interests — besides gaming, of course — can act as a conduit into finding a career.
 One career path for those with art skills — particularly with a talent for digital art tools like Autodesk Maya and Adobe Photoshop — is animation. An animator can do any number of jobs at a gaming company or studio, from building immersive landscapes and cities to modeling what a certain character will look like to designing user interfaces and navigational components. There is significant growth here, too: According to a  recent study, most sectors of the animation industry are growing 2% to 3% year over year. According to Gamesmith, the average 30-year-old female artist or animator makes a salary of just under $90,000 a year.
 If you’re a whiz with words and witty dialogue, then you might consider applying for a job as a writer at a studio. Writers are responsible for writing everything from the profanity-laced shouts heard in the background in the Grand Theft Auto series to the long speeches in epic adventures like The Legend of Zelda: Breath of the Wild. Employers are looking for writers with a flair for crafting stories and understanding characters, so even if you’re not a career writer, highlight the work you’ve done that fits in the mold of what game publishers are looking for.
 One of the most important segments of the industry — and one of the fastest-growing — is developing and designing the gaming experiences themselves. The job market for these roles is predicted to grow by 9.3% between 2016 and 2026,  according to the New England Institute of Technology , and the breadth of jobs in this wing of the industry range from level designers to lead designer and developers.
 Gamesmith calculated that these jobs account for 16% of the available openings, but make up only 5% of all applications. While you will need at least a computer science degree and an understanding of the fundamentals of programming to land one of these jobs, the payoff for the time spent hitting the books is worth it. Gamesmith estimates that the average 28-year-old male engineer earns a salary north of $100,000.
 Even if you don’t have any of these skills to help design a game from the ground up, there are still plenty of ways to break into the industry. No matter how good a game is, it will only be a success if people know about it, so the marketing and promotions teams at studios play a crucial role in making sure that consumers purchase the latest release and that it gets written about. If you have great communication skills and can work through people’s problems, companies always need customer service representatives.
 Across all sectors of the gaming world, companies are looking to diversify their workforce and move away from an image as a job sector solely populated by white men in their early to mid-30s. Gamesmith research found that currently 74% of the industry’s workforce is male and 64% is white.
 But that is changing. While in 2020, only 24% of studios invested moderate resources into diversity initiatives, out of those studios that did invest those resources, 96% reported at least moderately successful results and improvements to company culture. It may seem slow, but there does seem to be a recognition that the gaming industry needs a more diverse workforce as a way not just to bring more equity to their offices, but to make better games in the future and make the industry look more like the people who play games.
 “Diversity isn’t a nicety; it’s a necessity if the industry is going to grow, thrive and truly reflect the tens of millions of people who play games every day in this country,” said Jo Twist, the CEO of the U.K.-based gaming trade association Ukie. “A diverse industry that draws on myriad cultures, lifestyles and experiences will lead to more creative and inclusive games that capture the imagination of players and drive our sector forward.”
 Between the push to diversify the industry and a slew of new opportunities in the field, the key takeaway is that there are a wide range of possible careers in the industry and, even if you don’t think they do, your skills probably translate into one of the many roles that a gaming company needs to fill. Avid gamers know that you’re not going to beat a game the first time you turn on your console. So hone your skills, build up your experience and continue your quest to land a job in the industry of your dreams.

All that training, prep and focus leads to a final showdown and one last grab for the brass ring. And then it’s up to the judges to decide which stand-out startup wins the championship and that huge check.
 While only one startup wins the money and the title, every team that competes benefits from standing in a global spotlight. Sean Huang, co-founder of Matidor, competed in Startup Battlefield at Disrupt 2020. His team was  one of the five finalists. Here’s what he said about his experience:
 “Going through Startup Battlefield helped us simplify and improve our pitch. It helped us not only with brand messaging, but also to win other pitch competitions after Battlefield. By pitching in the finals, we booked a demo with one of the final panelists.
 We received inbound investment interest from 12 Tier-1 investors, and eight potential key clients came to our website for a demo session. We also received an endorsement letter for our Y Combinator application from a fellow Battlefield participant, with whom we formed a great connection.”
 You’re head down and focused — that’s why we’re giving you a one-week extension. So, stop, look up and grab this opportunity to take your startup to whole new levels. Get your nose off that grindstone and  apply to Startup Battlefield here before  May 27 at 11:59 pm (PT).

note_en:
Ireland’s national health service, the Health Service Executive (HSE), temporarily shut down its IT systems today after suffering a ransomware attack overnight.
 The organization, which is in the mid of its COVID-19 vaccination program, said the attack did not impact its ability to provide urgent medical care but that some routine checks and services might be delayed or canceled.
 The HSE described the ransomware incident as “significant” and “human-operated,” a term used to describe high-end sophisticated ransomware groups which orchestrate targeted attacks against carefully big organizations.
 In a  morning radio show with public broadcaster RTE, HSE Chief Executive Paul Reid said the agency’s IT teams are currently investigating the incident to find out its breadth.
 In a different radio show, Reid identified the ransomware gang behind the attack as   Conti, a ransomware gang that started operating in the summer of 2020.
 Paul Reid says the major ransomware attack targeting the HSE is "quite sophisticated", while the COVID-19 vaccination programme isn't impacted as it's on a different system. @NTBreakfast  pic.twitter.com/XXtzlzBQAV
— NewstalkFM (@NewstalkFM)  May 14, 2021
 Fergal Malone, Master of the Dublin Rotunda Hospital, told RTE in  a different interview that the attack impacted its staff’s ability to access online systems and patients’ electronic records.
 An HSE source told  The Record this morning that the attack came to the organization’s attention overnight after some of its apps and databases became inaccessible. Employees arriving at work in the morning were told to shut down and leave systems offline.
 Although the incident has been formally identified as a Conti ransomware attack, no ransom note has been currently discovered, although one is very likely to exist and that investigators have yet to find the file containing the attacker’s demands.
 HSE said its COVID-19 vaccination program remains on track, as it runs from a different system. The organization promised to share more details about the attack via its official channels, as they learn more.
 There is a significant ransomware attack on the HSE IT systems. We have taken the precaution of shutting down all our our IT systems in order to protect them from this attack and to allow us fully assess the situation with our own security partners.
— HSE Ireland (@HSELive)  May 14, 2021
 News of the attack comes after last week a ransomware gang managed to disrupt fuel delivery on the entire US East Coast through its  attack on Colonial Pipeline. The attack led the White House and major US institutions to look into cracking down on ransomware gangs who carry out disruptive attacks.
 In the aftermath of the Colonial attack, XSS, one of the forums where ransomware groups often go to advertise their offerings,  announced a ban on ransomware ads going forward, in an attempt to distance itself from ransomware groups, currently internet pariahs.

note_en:
Ireland's High Court on Friday rejected a Facebook bid to block an inquiry by the Irish data regulator that could halt Facebook's data flows from the European Union to the United States.
 "For the reasons set out in this judgment, I refuse all of the reliefs sought by FBI (Facebook Ireland) and dismiss the claims made by it in the proceedings," the court said in a judgement.
 "FBI has not established any basis for impugning the DPC’s (Data Protection Commissioner’s) decision or the PDD (Preliminary Draft Decision) or the procedures for the inquiry adopted by the DPC," the judgment said.

 这可能是由于在英格兰计算税收。在英国工资的各种类型税收中，到目前为止最大的税收是所得税。这是从一个人扣除的钱＆＃39;＆＃39;陛下＆＃39;收入和海关＆＃39; （俗称HMRC）甚至会击中他们的银行账户。根据您的薪水或预测收益的百分比取决于他们落入的税收括号，这项总额是制定的。 
英格兰＆＃39;税括号可以略微混乱;大多数人都有一个＆＃39;免税津贴＆＃39;约为12,500英镑。虽然金额可能不同于个人基础，但围绕它的概念是相同的;在前£12,500（或个人津贴的金额）没有税收。

 您还可以乘坐着名的黑色驾驶室，以及Uber，Gett或Kapten等应用。 
Google地图和CityMapper有助于查找路由，您还可以在手机上获取有关计划的更新信​​息。
 伦敦也是自行车友好的，您可以从不同的服务（例如，石灰或Santander周期）租一辆自行车或滑板车。
    您＆＃39; ll需要凭证地址证明以及银行通常要求的通常id文件。银行陈述通常被视为地址证明，但当然，如果它＆＃39;此后的第一个帐户搬迁，那么赢得了＆＃39;地址要求意味着您＆＃39; LL需要在您的房子或公寓中建立，所以如果您＆＃39;重新解决，它可能很棘手。我的解决方案正在使用Monzo，电子银行没有任何物理分支。您可以在线申请。您＆＃39; ll仍然需要英国地址以及您的身份证，但是您不需要留在您的永久居住的情况，所以您可以使用朋友＆＃39; spape或临时住所。您可以在一周内获得借记卡。

  你曾经在联合王国生活过了一年多半。您是否可以访问国家卫生系统（NHS）服务？
 是的，当您申请签证时，您支付NHS费用，因此您可以从您在英国抵达的第一天来访问作为公民的所有服务。
